HUMAN RESOURCES POLICY MANUAL
Section 5: Leave Policy

5.1 Annual Leave (Earned Leave):
a) All confirmed full-time employees are entitled to 15 days of earned leave per calendar year, accruing at 1.25 days per month.
b) Earned leave can be accumulated up to a maximum of 45 days. Leave in excess of 45 days will lapse at year end.
c) Application for earned leave must be submitted at least 5 working days in advance.
d) A minimum of 3 consecutive days must be availed at a time to qualify as earned leave.

5.2 Sick Leave:
a) Employees are entitled to 12 days of sick leave per calendar year, non-accumulative.
b) Sick leave beyond 2 consecutive days requires a medical certificate from a registered medical practitioner.
c) Sick leave cannot be carried forward to the next calendar year.

5.3 Casual Leave:
a) 6 days of casual leave per calendar year is provided for unforeseen personal needs.
b) Casual leave cannot be carried forward or en-cashed under any circumstances.
c) Casual leave cannot be combined with earned leave without prior approval.

5.4 Maternity Leave:
Women employees are entitled to 26 weeks of paid maternity leave for the first two surviving children, as per the Maternity Benefit Act, 1961 as amended in 2017.

5.5 Paternity Leave:
Male employees are entitled to 5 days of paid paternity leave to be taken within 30 days of childbirth.

TECHNOLOGY & BUSINESS

INDIAN FINTECH SECTOR RAISES RECORD $4.2 BILLION IN 2024

Mumbai, 15 January 2025 - India's financial technology sector reached a new milestone in 2024, attracting record investments of approximately $4.2 billion across 380 deals, according to a report released by DataWave Analytics on Tuesday.

The year saw particularly strong activity in payment infrastructure, lending technology, and insurance technology (InsurTech) segments. Payments emerged as the most funded segment with $1.8 billion in investments, reflecting the rapid expansion of UPI-based businesses and cross-border payment solutions.

Notable deals of the year included a $200 million Series D round by fintech startup CreditPulse and a $150 million investment in digital insurance platform PolicyBridge, both of which are backed by leading global venture capital firms.

"India's fintech ecosystem continues to mature rapidly," said Dr. Nikhil Rao, Chief Economist at the National Payments Corporation of India. "The combination of a large unbanked population, growing smartphone penetration, and supportive regulatory environment makes India one of the most attractive fintech markets globally."

Small business lending and rural financial inclusion emerged as the fastest-growing sub-segments, with 34 new startups launching products specifically targeting tier-2 and tier-3 city customers.

Industry observers expect continued growth in 2025, driven by the Government's Digital India mission and the Reserve Bank of India's progressive regulatory framework for new-age financial products.

INTERNAL AUDIT REPORT - SUMMARY
Audit Period: January 2025 to March 2025
Department Audited: Finance & Accounts
Audit Conducted By: Internal Audit Team
Date of Report: 10 April 2025

Objective:
To review compliance with company financial policies, assess internal controls, and identify areas of risk or improvement in the Finance & Accounts Department.

Key Findings:

1. Voucher Verification: Out of 1,450 vouchers reviewed, 22 vouchers (1.5%) were found without proper approval signatures. The department should enforce mandatory dual-authorization for all payments above Rs. 10,000.

2. Vendor Payments:
3 vendor payments totalling Rs. 4,52,000 were released without matching Purchase Orders. Immediate reconciliation is recommended.

3. Bank Reconciliation: Bank reconciliation statements for January 2025 were found pending. Monthly reconciliation must be completed within 7 working days of month end.

4. Fixed Asset Register: 
The fixed asset register was not updated for 18 new assets added in Q3. Immediate physical verification recommended.

Conclusion:
Overall, the financial controls are largely adequate. However, the above observations require prompt corrective action. The audit team requests a response action plan within 15 days.

HUMAN RESOURCES POLICY MANUAL Section 5: Leave Policy

5.1 Annual Leave (Earned Leave):
a) Entitlement: All confirmed full-time employees are entitled to 15 days of earned leave per calendar year, accruing at 1.25 days per month.
b) Accumulation: Earned leave can be accumulated up to a maximum of 45 days. Leave in excess of 45 days will lapse at year end.
c) Application: Application for earned leave must be submitted at least 5 working days in advance.
d)Minimum Duration: A minimum of 3 consecutive days must be availed at a time to qualify as earned leave.

5.2 Sick Leave:
a) Entitlement: Employees are entitled to 12 days of sick leave per calendar year, non-accumulative.
b) Documentation: Sick leave beyond 2 consecutive days requires a medical certificate from a registered medical practitioner.
c) Carry Forward: Sick leave cannot be carried forward to the next calendar year.

5.3 Casual Leave:
a) Entitlement: 6 days of casual leave per calendar year is provided for unforeseen personal needs.
b) Restrictions: Casual leave cannot be carried forward or en-cashed under any circumstances.
c) Combination: Casual leave cannot be combined with earned leave without prior approval.

5.4 Maternity Leave:
Women employees are entitled to 26 weeks of paid maternity leave for the first two surviving children, as per the Maternity Benefit Act, 1961 as amended in 2017.

5.5 Paternity Leave:
Male employees are entitled to 5 days of paid paternity leave to be taken within 30 days of childbirth.

TECHNOLOGY & BUSINESS

INDIAN FINTECH SECTOR RAISES RECORD $4.2 BILLION IN 2024

Mumbai, 15 January 2025 - India's financial technology sector reached a new milestone in 2024, attracting record investments of approximately $4.2 billion across 380 deals, according to a report released by DataWave Analytics on Tuesday.

The year saw particularly strong activity in payment infrastructure, lending technology, and insurance technology (InsurTech) segments. Payments emerged as the most funded segment with $1.8 billion in investments, reflecting the rapid expansion of UPI-based businesses and cross-border payment solutions.

Notable deals of the year included a $200 million Series D round by fintech startup CreditPulse and a $150 million investment in digital insurance platform PolicyBridge, both of which are backed by leading global venture capital firms.

"India's fintech ecosystem continues to mature rapidly," said Dr. Nikhil Rao, Chief Economist at the National Payments Corporation of India. "The combination of a large unbanked population, growing smartphone penetration, and supportive regulatory environment makes India one of the most attractive fintech markets globally."

Small business lending and rural financial inclusion emerged as the fastest-growing sub-segments, with 34 new startups launching products specifically targeting tier-2 and tier-3 city customers.

Industry observers expect continued growth in 2025, driven by the Government's Digital India mission and the Reserve Bank of India's progressive regulatory framework for new-age financial products.

Note 1: Significant Accounting Policies

a) Basis of Preparation: These financial statements have been prepared in accordance with the Indian Accounting Standards (Ind AS) as notified by the Ministry of Corporate Affairs (MCA) under the Companies (Indian Accounting Standards) Rules, 2015. The financial statements are prepared on historical cost basis.

b) Revenue Recognition: Revenue from the sale of goods is recognized when control of the asset is transferred to the customer, generally at the point of delivery. Revenue is measured at the transaction price agreed under the contract with the customer.

c) Property, Plant and Equipment: Property, plant and equipment are stated at cost less accumulated depreciation and impairment losses, if any. Cost includes purchase price, directly attributable costs, and estimated costs of dismantling.

d) Inventories: Inventories are valued at the lower of cost and net realizable value. Cost of raw materials is determined using the weighted average method.

e) Foreign Currency Transactions: Transactions in foreign currencies are translated at the exchange rates prevailing on the date of the transactions. Monetary assets and liabilities are translated at the rates prevailing on the balance sheet date.

Page #10 - Academic Research Paper Abstract
Words: 196

ABSTRACT

Title: Impact of Social Media Usage on Academic Performance Among Undergraduate Students: A Study of Selected Universities in Maharashtra

Authors: Dr. Supriya Joshi (1), Mr. Rahul Mishra (2)
(1) Associate Professor, Department of Sociology, SNDT Women's University, Mumbai
(2) Research Scholar, Department of Sociology, SNDT Women's University, Mumbai

Abstract: This study investigates the relationship between social media usage patterns and academic performance among undergraduate students enrolled in selected universities across Maharashtra. A stratified random sample of 450 students from five universities was surveyed using a structured questionnaire. Data was analyzed using SPSS software with correlation and regression analysis techniques.

Key Findings: Results indicate a statistically significant negative correlation (r = −0.48, p < 0.01) between excessive social media usage (more than 4 hours per day) and academic performance as measured by CGPA. Students who used social media primarily for academic purposes showed no significant impact on performance. The study also found that 72% of respondents accessed social media during study hours at least once daily.

Conclusion: The study recommends digital literacy programs, parental monitoring, and institutional guidelines to promote balanced social media usage among students.

Keywords: Social Media, Academic Performance, Undergraduate Students, Digital Literacy, Maharashtra

INTERNAL AUDIT REPORT - SUMMARY Audit Period: January 2025 to March 2025 Department Audited: Finance & Accounts Audit Conducted By: Internal Audit Team

Date of Report: 10 April 2025

Objective:

To review compliance with company financial policies, assess internal controls, and identify areas of risk or improvement in the Finance & Accounts Department.

Key Findings:

1. Voucher Verification:

Out of 1,450 vouchers reviewed, 22 vouchers (1.5%) were found without proper approval signatures. The department should enforce mandatory dual-authorization for all payments above Rs. 10,000.

2. Vendor Payments:

3 vendor payments totalling Rs. 4,52,000 were released without matching Purchase Orders. Immediate reconciliation is recommended.

3. Bank Reconciliation:

Bank reconciliation statements for January 2025 were found pending. Monthly reconciliation must be completed within 7 working days of month end.

4. Fixed Asset Register:

The fixed asset register was not updated for 18 new assets added in Q3. Immediate physical verification recommended.

Conclusion:

Overall, the financial controls are largely adequate. However, the above observations require prompt corrective action. The audit team requests a response action plan within 15 days.

TECHNOLOGY & BUSINESS

INDIAN FINTECH SECTOR RAISES RECORD $4.2 BILLION IN 2024

Mumbai, 15 January 2025 - India's financial technology sector reached a new milestone in 2024, attracting record investments of approximately $4.2 billion across 380 deals, according to a report released by DataWave Analytics on Tuesday.

The year saw particularly strong activity in payment infrastructure, lending technology, and insurance technology (InsurTech) segments. Payments emerged as the most funded segment with $1.8 billion in investments, reflecting the rapid expansion of UPI-based businesses and cross- border payment solutions.

Notable deals of the year included a $200 million Series D round by fintech startup CreditPulse and a $150 million investment in digital insurance platform PolicyBridge, both of which are backed by leading global venture capital firms.

"India's fintech ecosystem continues to mature rapidly," said Dr. Nikhil Rao, Chief Economist at the National Payments Corporation of India. "The combination of a large unbanked population, growing smartphone penetration, and supportive regulatory environment makes India one of the most attractive fintech markets globally."

Small business lending and rural financial inclusion emerged as the fastest-growing sub-segments, with 34 new startups launching products specifically targeting tier-2 and tier-3 city customers.

Industry observers expect continued growth in 2025, driven by the Government's Digital India mission and the Reserve Bank of India's progressive regulatory framework for new-age financial products.

Page #45 - Academic Research Paper Abstract
Words: 196

ABSTRACT

Title: Impact of Social Media Usage on Academic Performance Among Undergraduate Students: A Study of Selected Universities in Maharashtra

Authors: Dr. Supriya Joshi (1), Mr. Rahul Mishra (2) (1) Associate Professor, Department of Sociology, SNDT Women's University, Mumbai (2) Research Scholar, Department of Sociology, SNDT Women's University, Mumbai

Abstract:

This study investigates the relationship between social media usage patterns and academic performance among undergraduate students enrolled in selected universities across

Maharashtra. A stratified random sample of 450 students from five universities was surveyed using a structured questionnaire. Data was analyzed using SPSS software with correlation and regression analysis techniques.

Key Findings:

Results indicate a statistically significant negative correlation (r = -0.48, p < 0.01) between excessive social media usage (more than 4 hours per day) and academic performance as measured by CGPA. Students who used social media primarily for academic purposes showed no significant impact on performance. The study also found that 72% of respondents accessed social media during study hours at least once daily.

Conclusion:

The study recommends digital literacy programs, parental monitoring, and institutional guidelines to promote balanced social media usage among students.

Keywords: Social Media, Academic Performance, Undergraduate Students, Digital Literacy, Maharashtra

NOTES TO FINANCIAL STATEMENTS For the Year Ended 31 March 2025 Company: Bharat Consumer Products Ltd.

Note 1: Significant Accounting Policies

a) Basis of Preparation:

These financial statements have been prepared in accordance with the Indian Accounting Standards (Ind AS) as notified by the Ministry of Corporate Affairs (MCA) under the Companies (Indian Accounting Standards) Rules, 2015. The financial statements are prepared on historical cost basis.

b) Revenue Recognition:

Revenue from the sale of goods is recognized when control of the asset is transferred to the customer, generally at the point of delivery. Revenue is measured at the transaction price agreed under the contract with the customer

c) Property, Plant and Equipment:

Property, plant and equipment are stated at cost less accumulated depreciation and impairment losses, if any. Cost includes purchase price, directly attributable costs, and estimated costs of dismantling.

d) Inventories:

Inventories are valued at the lower of cost and net

realizable value. Cost of raw materials is determined using the weighted average method.

e) Foreign Currency Transactions:

Transactions in foreign currencies are translated at the exchange rates prevailing on the date of the transactions. Monetary assets and liabilities are translated at the rates prevailing on the balance sheet date.

TECHNOLOGY & BUSINESS

INDIAN FINTECH SECTOR RAISES RECORD $4.2 BILLION IN 2024

Mumbai, 15 January 2025 - India’s financial technology sector reached a new milestone in 2024, attracting record investments of approximately $4.2 billion across 380 deals, according to a report released by DataWave Analytics on Tuesday.

The year saw particularly strong activity in payment infrastructure, lending technology, and insurance technology (InsurTech) segments. Payments emerged as the most funded segment with $1.8 billion in investments, reflecting the rapid expansion of UPI-based businesses and cross-border payment solutions.

Notable deals of the year included a $200 million Series D round by fintech startup CreditPulse and a $150 million investment in digital insurance platform PolicyBridge, both of which are backed by leading global venture capital firms.

“India’s fintech ecosystem continues to mature rapidly,” said Dr. Nikhil Rao, Chief Economist at the National Payments Corporation of India. “The combination of a large unbanked population, growing smartphone penetration, and supportive regulatory environment makes India one of the most attractive fintech markets globally.”

Small business lending and rural financial inclusion emerged as the fastest-growing sub-segments, with 34 new startups launching products specifically targeting tier-2 and tier-3 city customers.

Industry observers expect continued growth in 2025, driven by the Government’s Digital India mission and the Reserve Bank of India’s progressive regulatory framework for new-age financial products.

Section 5: Leave Policy

5.1 Annual Leave (Earned Leave):
a) All confirmed full-time employees are entitled to 15 days of earned leave per calendar year, accruing at 1.25 days per month.
b) Earned leave can be accumulated up to a maximum of 45 days. Leave in excess of 45 days will lapse at year end.
c) Application for earned leave must be submitted at least 5 working days in advance.
d) A minimum of 3 consecutive days must be availed at a time to qualify as earned leave.

5.2 Sick Leave:
a) Employees are entitled to 12 days of sick leave per calendar year, non-accumulative.
b) Sick leave beyond 2 consecutive days requires a medical certificate from a registered medical practitioner.
c) Sick leave cannot be carried forward to the next calendar year.

5.3 Casual Leave:
a) 6 days of casual leave per calendar year is provided for unforeseen personal needs.
b) Casual leave cannot be carried forward or en-cashed under any circumstances.
c) Casual leave cannot be combined with earned leave without prior approval.

5.4 Maternity Leave:
Women employees are entitled to 26 weeks of paid maternity leave for the first two surviving children, as per the Maternity Benefit Act, 1961 as amended in 2017.

5.5 Paternity Leave:
Male employees are entitled to 5 days of paid paternity leave to be taken within 30 days of childbirth.

Out of 1,450 vouchers reviewed, 22 vouchers (1.5%) were found without proper approval signatures. The department should enforce mandatory dual-authorization for all payments above Rs. 10,000.

Vendor Payments:
3 vendor payments totalling Rs. 4,52,000 were released without matching Purchase Orders. Immediate reconciliation is recommended.

Bank Reconciliation:
Bank reconciliation statements for January 2025 were found pending. Monthly reconciliation must be completed within 7 working days of month end.

Fixed Asset Register:
The fixed asset register was not updated for 18 new assets added in Q3. Immediate physical verification recommended.

Conclusion:
Overall, the financial controls are largely adequate. However, the above observations require prompt corrective action. The audit team requests a response action plan within 15 days.

ABSTRACT

Title: Impact of Social Media Usage on Academic Performance Among Undergraduate Students: A Study of Selected Universities in Maharashtra

Authors: Dr. Supriya Joshi (1), Mr. Rahul Mishra (2)
(1) Associate Professor, Department of Sociology, SNDT Women’s University, Mumbai
(2) Research Scholar, Department of Sociology, SNDT Women’s University, Mumbai

Abstract:
This study investigates the relationship between social media usage patterns and academic performance among undergraduate students enrolled in selected universities across Maharashtra. A stratified random sample of 450 students from five universities was surveyed using a structured questionnaire. Data was analyzed using SPSS software with correlation and regression analysis techniques.

Key Findings:
Results indicate a statistically significant negative correlation (r = -0.48, p < 0.01) between excessive social media usage (more than 4 hours per day) and academic performance as measured by CGPA. Students who used social media primarily for academic purposes showed no significant impact on performance. The study also found that 72% of respondents accessed social media during study hours at least once daily.

Conclusion:
The study recommends digital literacy programs, parental monitoring, and institutional guidelines to promote balanced social media usage among students.

Keywords: Social Media, Academic Performance, Undergraduate Students, Digital Literacy, Maharashtra

TECHNOLOGY & BUSINESS

INDIAN FINTECH SECTOR RAISES RECORD $4.2 BILLION IN 2024

Mumbai, 15 January 2025 - India's financial technology sector reached a new milestone in 2024, attracting record investments of approximately $4.2 billion across 380 deals, according to a report released by DataWave Analytics on Tuesday.

The year saw particularly strong activity in payment infrastructure, lending technology, and insurance technology (InsurTech) segments. Payments emerged as the most funded segment with $1.8 billion in investments, reflecting the rapid expansion of UPI-based businesses and cross-border payment solutions.

Notable deals of the year included a $200 million Series D round by fintech startup CreditPulse and a $150 million investment in digital insurance platform PolicyBridge, both of which are backed by leading global venture capital firms.

"India's fintech ecosystem continues to mature rapidly," said Dr. Nikhil Rao, Chief Economist at the National Payments Corporation of India. "The combination of a large unbanked population, growing smartphone penetration, and supportive regulatory environment makes India one of the most attractive fintech markets globally."

Small business lending and rural financial inclusion emerged as the fastest-growing sub-segments, with 34 new startups launching products specifically targeting tier-2 and tier-3 city customers.

Industry observers expect continued growth in 2025, driven by the Government's Digital India mission and the Reserve Bank of India's progressive regulatory framework for new-age financial products.

ABSTRACT

Title: Impact of Social Media Usage on Academic Performance Among Undergraduate Students: A Study of Selected Universities in Maharashtra

Authors: Dr. Supriya Joshi (1), Mr. Rahul Mishra (2)
(1) Associate Professor, Department of Sociology, SNDT Women’s University, Mumbai
(2) Research Scholar, Department of Sociology, SNDT Women’s University, Mumbai

Abstract:
This study investigates the relationship between social media usage patterns and academic performance among undergraduate students enrolled in selected universities across Maharashtra. A stratified random sample of 450 students from five universities was surveyed using a structured questionnaire. Data was analyzed using SPSS software with correlation and regression analysis techniques.

Key Findings:
Results indicate a statistically significant negative correlation (r = -0.48, p < 0.01) between excessive social media usage (more than 4 hours per day) and academic performance as measured by CGPA. Students who used social media primarily for academic purposes showed no significant impact on performance. The study also found that 72% of respondents accessed social media during study hours at least once daily.

Conclusion:
The study recommends digital literacy programs, parental monitoring, and institutional guidelines to promote balanced social media usage among students.

Keywords: Social Media, Academic Performance, Undergraduate Students, Digital Literacy, Maharashtra

NOTES TO FINANCIAL STATEMENTS
For the Year Ended 31 March 2025
Company: Bharat Consumer Products Ltd.

Note 1: Significant Accounting Policies

a) Basis of Preparation:
These financial statements have been prepared in accordance with the Indian Accounting Standards (Ind AS) as notified by the Ministry of Corporate Affairs (MCA) under the Companies (Indian Accounting Standards) Rules, 2015. The financial statements are prepared on historical cost basis.

b) Revenue Recognition:
Revenue from the sale of goods is recognized when control of the asset is transferred to the customer, generally at the point of delivery. Revenue is measured at the transaction price agreed under the contract with the customer.

c) Property, Plant and Equipment:
Property, plant and equipment are stated at cost less accumulated depreciation and impairment losses, if any. Cost includes purchase price, directly attributable costs, and estimated costs of dismantling.

d) Inventories:
Inventories are valued at the lower of cost and net realizable value. Cost of raw materials is determined using the weighted average method.

e) Foreign Currency Transactions:
Transactions in foreign currencies are translated at the exchange rates prevailing on the date of the transactions. Monetary assets and liabilities are translated at the rates prevailing on the balance sheet date.

Small business lending and rural financial inclusion emerged as the fastest-growing sub-segments, with 34 new startups launching products specifically targeting tier-2 and tier-3 city customers.

Industry observers expect continued growth in 2025, driven by the Government's Digital India mission and the Reserve Bank of India's progressive regulatory framework for new-age financial products.

Funding Agency: National Rural Drinking Water (NRDWM) 

Excutive Summery:
The clean water initiative phase 2 has successfully provided safe and clean drinking water  access to 47 tribal  hamlets accross 12 gram panchayats in the drought-prone Barmer District. The project covered approximately 28,500 beneficiaries directly.

Page #9 - Academic Research Paper Abstract
Words: 191

ABSTRACT

Title: Impact of Social Media Usage on Academic  Performance Among Undergraduate Students:A Stdy of Selected Universities in Maharashta

Authors: Dr.Supriya Joshi (1), Mr. Rahul Mishra(2)
(1) Associate Professor, Department of Sociology, SNDT Women's university, Mumbai
(2) Research Scholar, Department of Sociology, SNDT Woman's university, Mumbai

Abstract:
This study investigates the reletionship between social media usage patterns and academic performance among undergraduate students enrolled in sekected universities across maharashta. A stratified random sample of 450 students from five universities was surveyed using a structured questionnaire, Data was analyzed using SPSS software with correlation and regression analysis techniques.

Key Findings:
Results indicate a statistically significant negative correlation (r = -0.48, p<0.01) between excessive social media usage )more than 4 hours per day) and Academic performance as measured by CGPA> Students who used social media primarily for academic purposes showed no significant impact on performance> The study also found that 72% of respondents accessed social media during study hours at least once daily.

Conclusion:
The study recommends digital literacy programs, parental monitoring, and institutional guidelines to promate balanced social media usage among students.

Keywords: Social Media, Academic Performannce, Undergraduate Students, digital Literacy, Maharashta

INTERNAL AUDIT REPORT - SUMMARY

Audit Period: January 2025 to March 2025

Department Audited: Finance &

Accounts

Audit Conducted By: Internal Audit Team Date of Report: 10 April 2025

Objective:

To review compliance with company financial policies, assess internal controls, and identify areas of risk or improvement in the Finance & Accounts Department.

Key Findings:

1. Voucher Verification:

Out of 1,450 vouchers reviewed, 22 vouchers (1.5%) were found without proper approval signatures. The department should enforce mandatory dual-authorization for all payments above Rs. 10,000.2. Vendor Payments:

3 vendor payments totalling Rs. 4,52,000 were released without matching Purchase Orders. Immediate reconciliation is recommended.

3. Bank Reconciliation: Bank reconciliation statements for January 2025 were found pending. Monthly reconciliation must be completed within 7 working days of month end.

4. Fixed Asset Register:

The fixed asset register was not updated for 18 new assets added in Q3. Immediate physical verification recommended.

Conclusion:

Overall, the financial controls are largely adequate. However, the above observations require prompt corrective action. The audit team requests a response action plan within 15 days.

NOTES TO FINANCIAL STATEMENTS
For the year Ended 31 March 2025
Company:Bharat Consumer Products Ltd. 

Note 1:Significant Accounting Policies

a) Basis of Preparation:
These financial statements have been prepared in accordance with the Indian Accounting Standards (Ind AS) as notified by the Ministry of Corporate Affairs (MCA) under the companies (Indian Accounting Standards) Rules, 2025. The financial statements are prepared on historical cost basis. 

b) Revenue Recognition:
Revenue from the sale of goods is recognized when control of the asset is transferred to the customer, generally at the point of delivery. Revenue is measured at the transaction price agreed under the contract with the customer. 

c) Property, Plant and Equipment:
Property, plant and equipment are started at cost less accumulated depreciation and impairment losses, if any. Cost includes purchase price, directly attributable costs, and estimated costs of dismantling. 

d) Inventories:
Inventories are valued at the lower of costand net realizable value. Cost of raw materials is determined using the weighted average method. 

e) Foreign Currency Transactions:
Transaction in foreign currencies are translated at the exchange rates prevailing on the date of the transactions. Monetary assets and liabilities are translated at the rates prevailing on the balance sheet date.

Date: 15 March 2025

To,

The Awards Committee CII (Confederation of Indian Industry) New Delhi Chapter

Sub: Nomination of Mr. Ashok Banerjee for the Young Entrepreneur Award 2025

Dear Committee Members,

I am honoured to nominate Mr. Ashok Banerjee, Founder and CEO of GreenPath Technologies Pvt. Ltd., Kolkata, for the prestigious CII Young Entrepreneur Award 2025.

About the Nominee:

Mr. Banerjee (aged 31) founded GreenPath Technologies in 2019 with a vision to provide affordable clean energy solutions to rural and semi-urban India.
Under his leadership, the company has:

1. Installed solar micro-grids in 127 villages across West Bengal and Bihar, providing electricity to over 85,000 households.

2. Created direct employment for 1,200 people, with 70% women employees.

3. Achieved revenue of Rs. 42 Crores in FY2024-25 with 65% year-on-year growth.

4. Won the National Renewable Energy Award 2024 from the Ministry of New and Renewable Energy.

5. Featured in Forbes India's 30 Under 30 List for 2024.

Mr. Banerjee exemplifies entrepreneurial vision, social impact, and sustainable business leadership. He is deeply committed to climate action and inclusive development.

I strongly recommend him for this prestigious recognition.

Name: Dr. P.K. Mishra
Former president, FICCI Eastern Region

INTERNAL AUDIT REPORT - SUMMARY

Audit Period: January 2025 to March 2025

Department Audited: Finance & Accounts

Audit Conducted By: Internal Audit Team

Date of Report: 10 April 2025

Objective:

To review compliance with company financial policies, assess internal controls, and identify areas of risk or improvement in the Finance & Accounts Department.

Key Findings:

1. Voucher Verification:

Out of 1,450 vouchers reviewed, 22 vouchers (1.5%) were found without proper approval signatures. The department should enforce mandatory dual-authorization for all payments above Rs. 10,000.

2. Vendor Payments:
3 vendor payments totalling Rs. 4,52,000 were released without matching Purchase Orders. Immediate reconciliation is recommended.

3. Bank Reconciliation:

Bank reconciliation statements for January 2025 were found pending. Monthly reconciliation must be completed within 7 working days of month end.

4. Fixed Asset Register:

The fixed asset register was not updated for 18 new assets added in Q3. Immediate physical verification recommended.

Conclusion:

Overall, the financial controls are largely adequate. However, the above observations require prompt corrective action. The audit team requests a response action plan within 15 days.

ABSTRACT

LTE+

59

Title: Impact of Social Media Usage on Academic Performance Among Undergraduate Students: A Study of Selected Universities in Maharashtra

Authors: Dr. Supriya Joshi (1), Mr. Rahul

Mishra (2)

(1) Associate Professor, Department of Sociology, SNDT Women's University, Mumbai

(2) Research Scholar, Department of Sociology, SNDT Women's University, Mumbai

Abstract:

This study investigates the relationship between social media usage patterns and academic performance among undergraduate students enrolled in selected universities across Maharashtra. A stratified random sample of 450 students from five universities was surveyed using a structured questionnaire. Data was analyzed using SPSS software with correlation and regression analysis techniques.

Key Findings:

Results indicate a statistically significant negative correlation (r = -0.48, p < 0.01) between excessive social media usage (more than 4 hours per day) and academic performance as measured by CGPA. Students who used social media primarily for academic purposes showed no significant impact on performance. The study also found that 72% of respondents accessed social media during study hours at least once daily.

Conclusion:

The study recommends digital literacy programs, parental monitoring, and institutional guidelines to promote balanced social media usage among students.

Keywords: Social Media, Academic Performance, undergraduate Students, Digital Literacy, Maharashtra

NOTES TO FINANCIAL STATEMENTS

For the Year Ended 31 March 2025 Company: Bharat Consumer Products Ltd.

Note 1: Significant Accounting Policies

a) Basis of Preparation:

These financial statements have been prepared in accordance with the Indian Accounting Standards (Ind AS) as notified by the Ministry of Corporate Affairs (MCA) under the Companies (Indian Accounting Standards) Rules, 2015. The financial statements are prepared on historical cost basis.

b) Revenue Recognition:

Revenue from the sale of goods is recognized when control of the asset is transferred to the customer, generally at the point of delivery. Revenue is measured at the transaction price agreed under the contract with the customer.
c) Property, Plant and Equipment:

Property, plant and equipment are stated at cost less accumulated depreciation and impairment losses, if any. Cost includes purchase price, directly attributable costs, and estimated costs of dismantling.

d) Inventories:

Inventories are valued at the lower of cost and net realizable value. Cost of raw materials is determined using the weighted average method.

e) Foreign Currency Transactions:

Transactions in foreign currencies are translated at the exchange rates prevailing on the date of the transactions. Monetary assets and liabilities are translated at the rates prevailing on the balance sheet date.

HUMAN RESOURCES POLICY MANUAL

Section 5: Leave Policy

5.1 Annual Leave (Earned Leave):

a) All confirmed full-time employees are entitled to 15 days of earned leave per calendar year, accruing at 1.25 days per month.

b) Earned leave can be accumulated up to a maximum of 45 days. Leave in excess of 45 days will lapse at year end.

c) Application for earned leave must be submitted at least 5 working days in advance.

d) A minimum of 3 consecutive days must be availed at a time to qualify as earned leave.

5.2 Sick Leave:

a) Employees are entitled to 12 days of sick leave per calendar year, non-accumulative.

b) Sick leave beyond 2 consecutive days requires a medical certificate from a registered medical practitioner.

c) Sick leave cannot be carried forwardto the next calendar year.

5.3 Casual Leave:

a) 6 days of casual leave per calendar year is provided for unforeseen personal needs.

b) Casual leave cannot be carried forward or en-cashed under any circumstances.

c) Casual leave cannot be combined with earned leave without prior approval.

5.4 Maternity Leave:

Women employees are entitled to 26 weeks of paid maternity leave for the first two surviving children, as per the Maternity Benefit Act, 1961 as amended in 2017.

5.5 Paternity Leave:

Male employees are entitled to 5 days of paid paternity leave to be taken within 30 days of childbirth.
